name: Vittra
description: Vittra are beings from Swedish folklore, closely tied to the land and its well-being. They are often described as small, human-like creatures who live underground, particularly beneath old trees, stones, and the foundations of buildings. They are generally invisible, but their presence can be felt or heard, and they are said to be able to take on human form. The vittra are deeply connected to the fortunes of farms and families, and it's crucial to maintain a good relationship with them. Disrespecting the vittra can lead to misfortune, illness, or even death, while treating them with respect and leaving offerings can bring good luck and prosperity. They are known to be easily offended and hold grudges, making it important to be mindful of their presence and avoid disturbing their homes. They are also believed to possess knowledge of hidden treasures and the secrets of the earth, and can sometimes be persuaded to share this knowledge with those who treat them well. They are a fundamental part of the Swedish folklore and landscape, a reminder of the deep connection between humans and nature.
subtitle: Guardians of the land and luck.
appearance: Vittra are typically invisible, but when they choose to appear, they resemble small, human-like figures, often dressed in grey or brown clothing that blends in with the natural surroundings. They may also appear as animals, such as cats or dogs, or even as shadows. Some accounts describe them as having wrinkled faces and long, flowing hair.
habitat: Vittra live underground, typically beneath old trees, large stones, and the foundations of buildings, especially old farms and barns. They are closely tied to the land and are most commonly found in rural areas.
behavior: Vittra are generally solitary creatures, but they can also live in small communities. They are known to be easily offended and hold grudges, but they can also be helpful and generous to those who treat them with respect. They are deeply connected to the fortunes of farms and families and can bring good luck or misfortune depending on how they are treated. They are also curious and enjoy observing humans.

keyMyths.title: The Buried Treasure
keyMyths.description: A farmer who treated the vittra with respect was rewarded with the knowledge of a hidden treasure buried on his land. He became wealthy and lived a long and prosperous life, always remembering to thank the vittra.

keyMyths.title: The Sick Cow
keyMyths.description: A farmer who neglected to leave offerings for the vittra found his cows falling ill one by one. Only after making amends and showing respect did the cows recover, teaching him a valuable lesson.

sources.author: Jan-Öjvind Swahn
sources.description: Collection of Nordic myths and legends.
sources.text: Collection of Nordic myths and legends.
